title: Affine Transformations
concept: An affine transformation combines translation, rotation, scaling, and shearing into one move, with a defining rule: parallel lines in the original image always stay parallel afterward.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What's the key difference between cropping and resizing?

options.text: Cropping discards content outside a region; resizing keeps everything but resamples it to new dimensions
They're two names for the exact same operation
Resizing only works on black-and-white images

exercises.explain: Right. Now let's combine several of our moves — translation, rotation, scaling, and one new one — into a single transform.

cards.title: Tilting a piece of text in a word processor
cards.body: Type a sentence, then hit "italic" — the letters lean sideways without getting taller, shorter, or spinning. That leaning move is called shearing, and combined with translation, rotation, and scaling, it forms a family of transforms called affine transformations.

cards.title: What is an affine transformation?
cards.body: An affine transformation is any combination of translation, rotation, scaling, and shearing applied together as one move. Its defining property: straight lines stay straight, and parallel lines stay parallel — even though angles and distances can change.

cards.title: Stretching a rubber grid
cards.body: Picture a square grid printed on a stretchy rubber sheet. Pull, twist, or slant the sheet and the squares can become rectangles, tilted parallelograms, or thinner shapes — but opposite sides of every cell always stay parallel to each other. That's exactly what an affine transform guarantees, no matter how you combine the moves.

cards.title: Straightening a slightly tilted scan
cards.body: A scanned page that came out a couple of degrees crooked can be fixed with an affine transform — rotate it back to level, maybe nudge it into frame. Because the page's edges were already roughly straight and parallel, a rotation-plus-translation affine fix is all that's needed.

cards.title: Affine can't fix every kind of distortion
cards.body: It's tempting to assume affine transforms can warp an image into any shape you want. They can't: because parallel lines are forced to stay parallel, affine transforms can never make lines converge or diverge — so they can't correct the kind of distortion you get from photographing something at a steep angle.

cards.title: What affine preserves
cards.body: The one rule that always holds, no matter how you combine the moves:

left.text:
A square grid
Sides parallel and equal

exercises.prompt: Which of these is the new move introduced alongside translation, rotation, and scaling to form affine transforms?
exercises.explain: Correct — shearing is the fourth ingredient, combined with translation, rotation, and scaling to make an affine transform.

exercises.prompt: True or false: After an affine transformation, lines that were parallel in the original image are always still parallel.
exercises.explain: True. Preserving parallel lines is the defining property of every affine transformation.

exercises.sentence: An affine transform can change angles and lengths, but parallel lines always stay ___.
exercises.explain: Parallel is correct — that's the one property every affine transform is guaranteed to preserve.

exercises.prompt: Which situation is a good fit for a simple affine fix?

options.text: A scanned document is rotated 3° off-level and needs to be straightened
A photo of a rectangular sign taken from a sharp side angle needs to be made to look front-on
A fisheye lens photo has curved, bulging lines that need to be made straight

exercises.explain: A small rotation to level out an already roughly-parallel page is exactly what an affine transform handles well.

exercises.prompt: You photograph a rectangular whiteboard from an angle, so in the photo its edges aren't parallel — they visibly converge toward one side, like train tracks in the distance. Why can't a purely affine transformation fix this?

options.text: Affine transforms always keep parallel lines parallel, so they cannot make converging lines spread back out into a true rectangle
Affine transforms can only be applied to text, not photos of physical objects
Affine transforms require the image to already be black-and-white
Affine transforms can only shrink an image, never straighten it

exercises.explain: Exactly right. Fixing converging lines needs more flexibility than affine allows. Next: perspective transformations — the tool built specifically for this job.
